did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifier with an RBF kernel to classify dermoscopic images of pigmented skin lesions into benign or malignant categories based on extracted shape, color, and texture features.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents an original algorithm for diagnosing skin lesions using feature extraction and SVM classification. It details a specific methodology (segmentation, feature extraction, normalization, classification), describes experimental results on a dataset of 120 images, and reports specific performance metrics (sensitivity 79.89%, specificity 90.03%, accuracy 84.09%). Although published in conference proceedings, it contains original experimental results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: A High Performance Algorithm to Diagnosis of Skin Lesions Deterioration in Dermatoscopic Images Using New Feature Extraction
journal: Proceeding of the IEEE 28th Canadian Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering
brief_summary: This study proposes a high-performance algorithm for diagnosing the deterioration of pigmented skin lesions in dermoscopic images, specifically distinguishing between benign and malignant (melanoma) cases. The method employs a four-step process involving segmentation, feature extraction based on the ABCD rule, normalization, and classification using a Support Vector Machine (SVM) with a Radial Basis Function (RBF) kernel. Features extracted include 12 shape features, 72 color features across five color spaces, and 35 texture features derived from gray-level images. The experimental evaluation was conducted on a dataset of 120 dermoscopic images (63 melanoma and 57 benign) from standard atlases. The proposed SVM-RBF classifier achieved a sensitivity of 79.89%, specificity of 90.03%, and accuracy of 84.09%, outperforming existing methods in the referenced literature.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the two classes in the binary classification task. The dataset contained 63 melanoma images out of 120 total images.
One of the two classes in the binary classification task. The dataset contained 57 benign images out of 120 total images.

all_task_types.target: Benign vs. Malignant (Melanoma) diagnosis of pigmented skin lesions
all_task_types.details: Binary classification task distinguishing between benign and malignant (melanoma) pigmented skin lesions using a Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifier with an RBF kernel. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories based on extracted shape, color, and texture features.

datasets_used.name: Standard Dermoscopic Atlases
datasets_used.description: A collection of 120 dermoscopic images of pigmented skin lesions obtained from two standard dermoscopic atlases referenced as [16] (http://www.dermis.net) and [17] (http://www.dermnet.com). The images are in JPEG format with sizes of 720x480 pixels.

use.training: Used for experimental evaluation of the proposed SVM-RBF classifier; specific split not reported.
use.testing: Used for experimental evaluation; performance metrics (sensitivity, specificity, accuracy) were calculated on this dataset.
use.other: The dataset was used to compare the proposed method's performance against existing methods from references [7] and [8].

statistical_methods_used.method: Support Vector Machine (SVM) with Radial Basis Function (RBF) kernel
statistical_methods_used.purpose: Classification of pigmented skin lesions into benign or malignant categories based on extracted shape, color, and texture features.

statistical_methods_used.method: Comparison of SVM kernels (linear, polynomial, RBF, MLP)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate and select the optimal kernel for classification performance based on sensitivity, specificity, and accuracy.
To report the average performance metrics (sensitivity, specificity, accuracy) of the SVM classification to ensure reliability.
To handle different value ranges of extracted features before classification.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using sensitivity, specificity, and accuracy metrics averaged over 30 executions, and compared qualitatively to existing methods.

Standard Dermoscopic Atlases (120 images.notes: Results are averages of 30 executions. The SVM with RBF kernel outperformed other kernels and existing methods [7] and [8]. Method [7] used 7 features on 50 images. Method [8] used wavelet transformation and ABCD rule.

feature_extraction.texture_method: Gray-Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM) and one-level wavelet transform

preprocessing_techniques: Color space conversion to YUV for segmentation
Denoising of the U channel using low-pass Fourier-domain filtering
Morphological reconstruction algorithms for post-processing segmentation
Tracing binary segmented image onto original dermoscopic image to isolate lesion regions
Denoising of gray-level original dermoscopic image for texture feature extraction
Normalization of extracted features to range [-1, 1]
Haar wavelet transform applied to gray-level lesion image for texture feature extraction
conclusions: The proposed feature extraction-based algorithm, utilizing 12 shape, 72 color, and 35 texture features normalized and classified by an SVM with an RBF kernel, achieved a sensitivity of 79.89%, specificity of 90.03%, and accuracy of 84.09%.
The SVM classifier with the Radial Basis Function (RBF) kernel outperformed other tested kernels (linear, polynomial, and MLP) in terms of accuracy, specificity, and sensitivity.
The proposed method's diagnostic accuracy (84.09%) improved upon existing methods referenced in the study, which reported accuracies of 76% and 84%.

The study demonstrates that computerized diagnosis using dermoscopic images can help reduce unnecessary biopsies and associated morbidity by improving the differentiation between benign and malignant pigmented skin lesions.
important_remarks: The study uses a very small dataset of only 120 dermoscopic images (63 melanoma, 57 benign) sourced from two standard atlases, which limits statistical power and generalizability.
The dataset is drawn from standard dermoscopic atlases rather than a clinical cohort, introducing potential selection bias regarding image quality, lesion distribution, and patient demographics.
No external validation was performed; the model was evaluated solely on the internal dataset, raising concerns about overfitting and real-world clinical applicability.

The paper does not report a specific train/test/validation split or cross-validation strategy, only stating that results are averages of 30 executions, which may obscure data leakage or over-optimistic performance estimates.
Formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was not conducted to validate the significance of the performance improvements over baseline methods.

The comparison with existing methods [7] and [8] is indirect, as those studies used different datasets (e.g., method [7] used 50 images) and feature sets, making direct performance comparison difficult.
The algorithm relies on manual segmentation steps (Otsu thresholding, morphological reconstruction) which can introduce variability and error propagation into the feature extraction process.

The study is a conference paper published in 2015, using older technology (MATLAB R2011a) and traditional machine learning (SVM) rather than deep learning, which may limit its relevance compared to more recent state-of-the-art approaches.
